evidence.title: Back pain
evidence.snippet: take anti-inflammatory medicine like ibuprofen – paracetamol on its own is not recommended for back pain but it may be used with another painkiller

evidence.title: Management of Acute Musculoskeletal Pain - Clinical Practice Guideline | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Topical NSAIDs, with or without menthol gel, should be used as first line therapy for adults with acute pain from non-low back, musculoskeletal injuries.

evidence.title: Costochondritis: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Talk with your provider before using these medicines if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, liver disease, or have had stomach ulcers or internal bleeding in the past. Take the dose as advised by the provider. Do not take more than the amount recommended on the bottle. Carefully read the warnings on the label before taking any medicine. You may also take acetaminophen (Tylenol) instead, if your provider tells you it is safe to do so. People with liver disease should not take this medicine.

evidence.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Acute Pain | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Pharmacologic management of acute pain should be tailored for each patient, including a review of treatment expectations and a plan for the time course of prescriptions. Acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are first-line treatment options for most patients with acute mild to moderate pain. Topical NSAIDs are recommended for non–low back, musculoskeletal injuries. Acetaminophen is well tolerated; however, lower doses should be used in patients with advanced hepatic disease, malnutrition, or severe alcohol use disorder. Nonselective NSAIDs are effective but should be used with caution in patients with a history of gastrointestinal bleeding, cardiovascular disease, or chronic renal disease. Selective cyclooxygenase-2 NSAIDs are a more expensive treatment alternative and are used to avoid the gastrointestinal adverse effects of nonselective NSAIDs. Adjunctive medi

evidence.title: Slipping rib syndrome: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Talk with your provider before using these medicines if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, liver disease, or have had stomach ulcers or internal bleeding in the past. Take the dose as advised by your provider. Do not take more than the amount recommended on the bottle or advised by your provider. Carefully read the warnings on the label before taking any medicine. Your provider may also prescribe pain medicines to relieve pain.

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull aching pain localized to the left side of the lower rib cage. She denies any recent injury, trauma, or heavy lifting prior to onset. The pain improves with good posture but worsens when leaning against the area while sitting. She has no associated fever, chills, night sweats, numbness, weakness, tingling, or changes in bowel or bladder function. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of cancer, unexplained weight loss, or osteoporosis. She denies shortness of breath, cough, or chest pain.

O: Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal pain likely related to soft tissue strain or minor muscle injury in the left lower rib cage area.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or myofascial pain
2. Costochondritis
3. Intercostal nerve irritation or neuropathy
4. Early presentation of visceral pathology such as pleuritis or referred pain from abdominal organs

P: First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily with food for 7 days
Alternatives: Diclofenac 50 mg PO three times daily with food for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing), Meloxicam 15 mg PO once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
Medication safety considerations: Use with caution in patients with gastrointestinal risk factors; advise taking with food to minimize GI irritation. Monitor for any signs of allergic reaction or worsening symptoms.
Return precautions: Return or seek immediate care if patient develops fever, worsening pain, new neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), changes in bowel or bladder function, or if pain does not improve after 7 days of treatment.
Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flag symptoms; consider imaging or further workup if symptoms worsen or fail to improve.
